coredata.dc:title: Engineering luminescent pectin-based hydrogel for highly efficient multiple sensing
coredata.dc:creator: Tang, Karen Yuanting
Jiang, Lu
Yeo, Jayven Chee Chuan
Owh, Cally
Ye, Enyi
Loh, Xian Jun
Li, Zibiao
coredata.dc:description: Luminescent hydrogels with sensing capabilities have attracted much interest in recent years, especially those responsive to stimuli, making such materials potential for various applications. Pectin is a high-molecular-weight carbohydrate polymer that has the ability to form hydrogel upon heating or mixing with divalent cations. However, intrinsic pectin gels are weak and lack of functionalities. In this study, lanthanide ions and silk fibroin derived carbon dots were incorporated into Pectin/PVA hydrogel (PPH) to form luminescent tough hydrogels. The luminescence of the hydrogel can be tuned by adjusting the ratio of blue emission carbon dots to Eu3+ ions (red emission) and Tb3+ ions (green emission). Such incorporation of emitters only slightly changed the mechanical properties of the tough hydrogel. Notably, the luminescent Pectin/PVA hydrogel (LPPH) showed chromic response to external stimuli, like pH and metal ions. By measuring the ratio of luminescent intensity at 473 nm and 617 nm (I473/I617), the pH response can be quantified in high sensitivity. In addition, the specific detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions using the fabricated hydrogel were demonstrated, the mechanism was also proposed. The different chromic responses to Fe2+ and Fe3+ endow the luminescent tough Pectin/PVA hydrogel potential for multiple sensing applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Pectin hydrogel
Integrated luminescent emitters
Chromic response
Sensing application
Stimuli-responsive

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pglast pii piinorm pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure table body affil articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast primabst ref

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of PPH and LPP hydrogels

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration showing the luminescent sensing of tough Pectin/PVA hydrogel (LPPH) by different metal ions.
Spectroscopic characterization of CDs and GMA modified CDs: a) FTIR (inset: zoom-in) and b) 1H NMR spectra of CDs and GMA-CDs (inset: molecular structure and STEM images).
PL emission spectra under different excitation wavelength for a) CDs and b) GMA-CDs. Inserted photographs were taken under UV radiation at 365 nm.

PL spectra of the LPPH hydrogels incorporated with different emitters excited at 370 nm (inset: photographs showing the hydrogels incorporated with different emitters taken under UV radiation at 365 nm).

Rheological behaviour of a) PPH and b) LPPH hydrogels with a temperature sweep at +0.5 °C min−1. c) DSC thermograms of first heating cycle for PPH and LPPH hydrogels (inset: Zoom-in of PPH). Arrows indicate endothermic signals of crosslink dissociation.
PL spectra of the LPPH hydrogel at different pH values. The peak intensity ratios of I473/I617 are presented in brackets. Insert photographs show the pH-responsive LPPH hydrogel.
PL spectra of LPPH hydrogel in response to different metal ions. Metal ions responsiveness of LPPH hydrogel. Insert photographs show the metal ions responsive LPPH hydrogels.
Mechanical characteristics of as-synthesized PPH and LPPH hydrogels.

head.ce:title: Engineering luminescent pectin-based hydrogel for highly efficient multiple sensing

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, Agency for Science, Technology, and Research (A*STAR), 2 Fusionopolis Way, Innovis, 138634, Singapore

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering
Agency for Science, Technology, and Research (A*STAR)

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Materials Research and Engineering, Agency for Science, Technology, and Research (A*STAR), 2 Fusionopolis Way, Innovis, Singapore 138634

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Luminescent hydrogels with sensing capabilities have attracted much interest in recent years, especially those responsive to stimuli, making such materials potential for various applications. Pectin is a high-molecular-weight carbohydrate polymer that has the ability to form hydrogel upon heating or mixing with divalent cations. However, intrinsic pectin gels are weak and lack of functionalities. In this study, lanthanide ions and silk fibroin derived carbon dots were incorporated into Pectin/PVA hydrogel (PPH) to form luminescent tough hydrogels. The luminescence of the hydrogel can be tuned by adjusting the ratio of blue emission carbon dots to Eu3+ ions (red emission) and Tb3+ ions (green emission). Such incorporation of emitters only slightly changed the mechanical properties of the tough hydrogel. Notably, the luminescent Pectin/PVA hydrogel (LPPH) showed chromic response to external stimuli, like pH and metal ions. By measuring the ratio of luminescent intensity at 473 nm and 617 nm (I473/I617), the pH response can be quantified in high sensitivity. In addition, the specific detection of Cu2+ and Fe3+ ions using the fabricated hydrogel were demonstrated, the mechanism was also proposed. The different chromic responses to Fe2+ and Fe3+ endow the luminescent tough Pectin/PVA hydrogel potential for multiple sensing applications.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent hydrogels had gained interest recently due to their potential applications in sensing, energy and display [1–10]. They allow more versatility in optical tunability [1] and have a higher sensitivity to stimuli responsiveness [4]. One of the strategies to fabricate these white light-emitting hydrogel is by incorporating three primary colored (red, blue, green) luminescent dyes into the hydrogel matrix [1]. For example, Benson and co-workers [11] reported the use of Coumarin 460 (blue), fluorescein (green) and 5(6)-carboxy-x-rhodamine (red) in bovine serum albumin to form an inexpensive and efficient White-light emitting (WLE) hydrogel. Upon excitation at 365 nm, white emission was obtained via Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), through specific ratios of the three dyes.

One of the most promising luminescence emitters is lanthanide ions, due to their superior optical properties and resistance to photobleaching [12]. Europium (Eu3+) and terbium (Tb3+) ions are commonly used in luminescence engineering, as they have distinct red and green emissions respectively [4]. Furthermore, the use of an adjacent blue-light emitting “antenna” dye enables the possibility of luminescent hydrogel upon energy transfer to the lanthanide ions. For instance, Chen et al. fabricated the organometallic gels [4]. by coordinating terpyridyl-end-capped four-armed poly(ethylene glycol) polymer with Eu3+ and Tb3+. Various colored luminescent hydrogels were obtained with careful tuning of the ratios of the ions. On separate cases, Kumar's group used different donors (naphthalene, phenanthrene and pyrene) with Eu3+ and Tb3+ in poly(aryl ether) to obtain a WLE gel [2]. However, these WLE hydrogels were synthesized in organic solvents which lower the environmental friendliness, thus, limiting the range of potential applications.

Incorporation of a blue-light emitter is one of the strategies to fabricate luminescent hydrogel together with the usage of Eu3+ and Tb3+ (red and green emission). Carbon dots (CDs) are a promising candidate for water-soluble blue-light emitter, as they are also superior in various aspects, like good resistance to photo-bleaching, good chemical stability and low toxicity [13,14]. CDs are also known to be responsive to stimuli like copper and ferric ions [15,16], which imparts unique sensing capabilities to the hydrogel. Zhu and co-workers [17] incorporated Eu3+, Tb3+ and CDs into a network of polyacrylamide and poly(acrylic acid) to obtain a WLE hydrogel that is multi stimuli-responsive. However, the acid sites of the polymer network interact with the stimuli and cause the deformation of the hydrogel.

Recently we reported a tough hydrogel based on ionic pectin and poly(vinyl alcohol) (PVA) for implantable remote and controlled release device [18]. Both pectin and PVA alone have shown to be biocompatible [19] and favorable candidates for pharmaceutical uses [20]. The tough hydrogel was fabricated through the interpenetration of a primary PVA network prepared via cyclic freeze-thaw process and a secondary ionic calcium-pectin network by immersion of salt bath. In this work, we incorporated Eu3+, Tb3+ and CD into an interpenetrating network of pectin and PVA to form a tough luminescent hydrogel LPPH, which is responsive to various stimuli without any deformation. The high sensitivity of LPPH hydrogel to a wide spectrum of metal ions, together with their green fabrication process without using any organic solvents make LPPH gel promising in environmental sensing applications (Fig. 1).

Glycidyl methacrylate (GMA), polyvinyl alcohol (PVA) (MW 130,000, 99+% hydrolysed), pectin (P) from citrus peel (Product No.: P9135, galacturonic acid ≥74%), polyethyleneimine (PEI) (branched, MW 800 by LS), europium (III) chloride hexahydrate, terbium (III) chloride hexahydrate and 2,2′:6′,2″-terpyridine were purchased from Sigma Aldrich. All chemicals were used as received without further purification. Cocoon of Bombyx mori Silk were purchased from a silk farm and degummed in lab according to our previous work [21]. In all synthesis and immersion steps, deionised (DI) water (18.2 MΩ) was used.

The CDs were synthesized in accordance to our previous work [21]. In a typical experiment, 1 g of degummed silk fibre was added with 25 mL of water into a hydrothermal vessel and heated at 200 °C for 72 h. The mixture was then filtered and lyophilized in a freeze-dryer to obtain solid CDs.

The purified CDs were then methacrylated with GMA. In brief, 0.5 g of dried CDs was dissolved in 5 mL of DI water with sonication, 1 mL of GMA was then added and stirred for 24 h at 30 °C. Next, the oil phase was discarded, and the water phase was washed for 5 times with n-hexane to remove the excess GMA. The solution was then lyophilized in a freeze-dryer to obtain solid GMA-CDs for further use.

The Pectin/PVA hydrogel (PPH) was fabricated according to our previous report [18]. PVA was added into an aqueous solution of pectin with a ratio of 8:1, with polymer concentration of 15% (w/v) at 95 °C. This solution was poured into respective moulds and subjected to three freeze-thaw cycles. In the first freeze-thaw cycle (1FT), solution was cured at −20 °C for 71 h and then left at room temperature for 1 h. The additional freeze-thaw cycles (2FT and 3FT) were done at −20 °C for 23 h and then left at room temperature for 1 h. Hydrogels were then stored in the fridge at 2 °C for further use.

An equal amount of GMA-CDs and PEI (0.2 g each) were dissolved 20 mL of DI water. The mixture was then stirred at 95 °C for 24 h. The solution was then topped up to 200 mL to obtain a PEI-GMA-CD solution. The Pectin/PVA hydrogel was fully immersed in PEI-GMA-CD solution for 24 h, followed by immersion in DI water for 24 h to remove unreacted PEI-GMA-CD. The hydrogel was then immersed in the lanthanide solution, containing 10 mM EuCl3 and 10 mM TbCl3 for 24 h, then washed with DI water for 24 h to remove uncoordinated lanthanide ions. Finally, LPPH hydrogel was obtained by the final immersion of the gel in 0.2 mM terpyridine for 24 h, then washed with DI water for 24 h.

The FTIR analysis of CDs and GMA-CDs were performed using Perkin Elmer Spectrum 2000 with the ATR measurement mode, on zinc selenide crystal. It is done in ambient conditions with 4 cm−1 a resolution within 4000–400 cm−1 for 64 scans.
1H NMR was conducted on CDs and GMA-CDs in D2O solvent using a JEOL 500 MHz NMR System (JNM-ECA500IIFT). The residual protonated solvent signals were used as reference.
Size and morphology of CDs and GMA-CDs were studied with images captured using FEI Titan TEM in the Scanning Transmission Electron Microscopy (STEM) mode.

All photoluminescence (PL) spectra were obtained using RF-5301PC Flurophotospectrometer from Shimadzu Co. Ltd. Japan. The samples were excited to 370 nm with an emission slit size 3 and excitation slit size 5.

Tensile tests were conducted using an Instron 5569 universal testing machine with video extensometer and 100 N load cell. Samples were prepared into a dog-bone shape (length > 10.0 mm, width = 3.2 mm) using a die cutter, and pulled at a crosshead speed of 50 mm min−1 until fracture. To ensure consistent testing, only samples with thickness values around 2 (±0.2) mm were used.

The thermal properties of hydrated gels were characterized by differential scanning calorimeter (DSC) on a photo differential scanning calorimeter (PDSC, Q100, TA Instruments, USA) equipped with an auto cool accessory and calibrated using indium. For DSC analysis, hydrated gels were first cut into small pieces and loaded into hermetically sealed pans. Tests were then operated at a rate of 5 °C min−1 from 10 to 110 °C.

Rheology analysis of the hydrogels was conducted on a Discovery DHR-3 hybrid rheometer from TA Instruments using a flat parallel steel plate geometry with a diameter of 20 mm at a gap of 2 mm. The hydrated gels were cut into round shaped pieces having the same size with geometry used. Temperature ramp test was performed to investigate the mechanical property changes of hydrogels along with the increase of temperature. For temperature ramp, the test was conducted at a strain of 0.1% (well within their respective linear viscoelastic regions (LVR)), frequency of 1 Hz, and a heating rate of 0.5 °C min−1 from 10 to 100 °C. A thin layer of mineral oil was applied to the sample surface prior to test to prevent water loss.

STEM was used to characterize size and morphology changes after the modification reactions. As shown in Fig. 2b, one can see both CDs and GMA-CDs are spherical in shape with similar size of 10–25 mm. However, it can be observed that while CDs are highly dispersed, whereas the GMA-CDs tend to agglomerate easily. The agglomeration of GMA-CDs is probably due to the enhanced interaction of the organic chains at dry state of each modified particle. In addition, CDs and GMA-CDs have similar PL properties such as maximum absorption and emission wavelength and tunable emission under different excitations (Fig. 3 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of PPH and LPP hydrogels
ce:section.ce:para: The PPH hydrogel was first prepared by subjecting the solution of pectin and PVA (1:8 ratio) to cyclic freeze-thawing to initiate PVA crystallite formation. The fabricated Pectin/PVA hydrogel is slightly yellowish due to the pectin content, and opaque from the crystallite formation. Due to the opacity and yellowish tint, separate PPH hydrogels were subjected to immersion of different emitters first to check for their PL properties. The CD-doped hydrogel was fabricated by immersion in PEI-GMA-CD solution. As the PPH hydrogel has many free carboxylic acid sites, allowing the functionalized GMA-CDs to attach to the hydrogel through electrostatic interaction. To do so, GMA-CDs were first reacted with PEI, via a 1,4-Micheal addition, to obtain PEI-GMA-CD. A branched PEI with low molecular weight is chosen as it could allow more penetration into the hydrogel matrix due to its smaller hydrodynamic diameter. It was then diluted to the required concentration to endow effective migration into the hydrogel during immersion. The amine groups in PEI then reacted with the carboxylic groups in pectin of the PPH hydrogel. The lanthanide-doped hydrogels were obtained by simply immersing the PPH hydrogels into the respective lanthanide solutions followed by terpyridine solution. The lanthanide ions were easily attached to the carboxylic acid groups in the PPH hydrogel as illustrated in the PL spectra of hydrogel. The antenna and terpyridine would then interact with the lanthanide ions. Finally, the LPPH hydrogel was fabricated from a series of immersions of the PPH hydrogel in different emitters and antenna solutions and the incorporation of each component was monitored by the PL spectra of different hydrogels (Fig. 4 ).

In Fig. 4, the PL spectra shows the following signals: 491 nm (Tb3+, 5D4 ➔ 7F6), 545 nm (Tb3+, 5D4 ➔ 7F5), 586 nm (a combination of Tb3+, 5D4 ➔ 7F4 & Eu3+, 5D0 ➔ 7F2) and 617 nm (a combination of Tb3+, 5D4 ➔ 7F3 and Eu3+, 5D0 ➔ 7F2). It can also be observed as a broad peak at 473 nm corresponding to the characteristic of CDs' blue light. The intensities of all peaks also decrease tremendously in LPPH hydrogel, when compared to individually doped hydrogels. This phenomenon arises due to the enhanced “inner filter effect”, causing an overall increase in UV absorption. The addition of CDs results in more shallow UV penetration and decreased excitation of CDs and terpyridine, which also decreased the excitation of Eu3+ and Tb3+, leading to the overall decreased luminescence observed in LPPH hydrogel.

The mechanical properties of PPH and LPPH hydrogel were further evaluated by tensile tests (Table 1 ). As the PPH hydrogel does not contain forms of coordinating ions/compounds, it has an overall tighter polymer network, making the hydrogel stiffer and has a higher maximum tensile strength. However, after the addition of the emitters, the interactions between the emitter ions and the carboxylic acids led a secondary crosslinking in the hydrogel that caused a toughening effect of the as hydrogels.

Rheological temperature sweep tests were done on both PPH and LPPH hydrogels, showing that they both exhibits high initial storage moduli at 104–105 Pa range, but weaken substantially with the increase of temperature (Fig. 5a and b). According to the curves of loss factor tan δ, multiple softening steps starting from 39.6 °C were observed for the PPH hydrogel, whereas a distinct softening peak of LPPH hydrogel was only observed at 63.7 °C. The maxima in tan δ are observed with further heating at 90.9 °C and 83.7 °C for PPH and LPPH hydrogels, respectively. Further heating also leads to decrease in the storage moduli for both materials. Compared to PPH hydrogel, which shows only 1 magnitude dropping, the decrease for LPPH hydrogel is more drastic, dropping by 2 magnitudes to less than 103 Pa. The initial softening observed of PPH hydrogel was a result of the partial dissociation of the crosslinks. As the temperature increases, total dissociation occurs in both hydrogels and the storage moduli of both hydrogels decreases. The crystallites of lower thermal stability (seen in Fig. 5a) may be disrupted by the different emitters [18], hence, softening peaks were not seen for LPPH hydrogel (Fig. 5b). The more drastic decease of the storage modulus of the LPPH hydrogel may due to more destruction in the network, including total dissociation of the emitters.

Furthermore, the storage modulus of LPPH hydrogel decreases below the loss modulus between 80.9 °C to 86.6 °C, making it has a more fluid-like property during this temperature range. One explanation for this temporary fluid-like property is due to the repulsion of the polymer charges, the network became non-interpenetrating and thus, exhibits more fluid-like properties. However, when the temperature increases, the random movement of the polymer strands causes entanglement and form interpenetrating network again and has solid-like properties again. Overall, the mechanical property and temperature resistance of LPPH hydrogel are slightly weaker compared to PPH hydrogel, this may because crosslinks of hydrogels disrupted by incorporating emitters.

The DSC curves (Fig. 5c) show multiple endothermic signals from the 1st heating cycle for PPH and LPPH hydrogels, which also compliment the rheology results. Using the leftmost signal on each thermogram as an “onset” temperature, PPH hydrogel have onset temperature at around 39 °C, while onset temperature of LPPH hydrogel is at around 45 °C. However, the PPH hydrogel thermogram show more signals across a wider range of temperature, which is linked to crystallite melting [22,23] and glass transition [24,25] of PVA. The larger range of temperature also indicates that there is a larger crystallite size distribution [26]. The huge endothermic signal around 81.5 °C in the LPPH hydrogel thermogram further demonstrates the deformation of LPPH hydrogel and its fluid-like property between 81 °C to 87 °C.

Due to the unique physical-chemical properties of the emitters (Tb3+, Eu3+ and CDs), the obtained LPPH hydrogel can be responsive to various stimuli, creating various sensing applications. In this paper, two of the multiple stimuli, pH and metal ions, were investigated and discussed. Firstly, the responsiveness of the LPPH hydrogel towards pH was investigated. The hydrogels showed colorimetric change at different pH condition (Fig. 6 ), remaining white at pH 7 but shifts to blue when solutions are acidic/basic. The PL intensity ratios of the hydrogel at I473/I617 showed a significant change at different pH values. This is unique to systems using similar emitters, due to the different blends of emitters at different pH [4,27]. In most single-chromophore pH sensors, however, only see a change in color at the pKa of their chromophore [28]. Even though there is a white-to-blue shift for both acidic and basic conditions, it is dominated by different mechanisms in acid and base respectively [17]. At acidic conditions, the protonation of terpyridine causes the dissociation of the lanthanide-terpyridine complex, weakening the emission from the lanthanide ions. The pKa of terpyridine is at 3.57 and 4.54, hence, there is a complete dissociation at pH 3, making the white-to-blue shift very distinct. At basic conditions, the white-to-blue shift is, however, caused by the formation of insoluble lanthanide hydroxide, leading to a dissociation of the terpyridine-lanthanide complex. Regardless of acidic or basic conditions, the I473/I617 ratio (blue to red/green emission) can be used as a quantitative index of pH response for the LPPH hydrogel's sensing. There might be significant swelling due to the strong repulsion force between the free carboxylate groups, resulted from the loss of lanthanide ions [17]. However, the LPPH hydrogel presented in this study did not deform/swell, irrespective of acidic or basic conditions. This can be attributed to a few reasons. Firstly, the as-synthesized PPH hydrogel has very good mechanical properties which is not very far off from our LPPH hydrogel (Table 1). The repulsion force does not have significant energy to overcome them to create a visible swell/deformation. Secondly, the interpenetrating network of the PVA and pectin does not allow the polymer to be far apart, even when there is a strong repulsion. Hence, swelling/deformation was not observed.

Next, the responsiveness of the LPPH hydrogel towards various metal ions, including alkali and transition metals. Fig. 7 shows the responsiveness of the LPPH hydrogel after a 30-minute immersion of 10 mM of metal ions. The LPPH hydrogels remain unchanged for alkali and alkaline earth metals (Na+, K+, Ca2+ and Mg2+), indicating that no replacement of coordinating ions had occurred. Meanwhile, the WLE hydrogels have certain responses to all the transition metals tested. This is due to the binding selectivity of the ions towards terpyridine. The transition metal-terpyridine complex is more stable than the lanthanide-terpyridine complex [29,30], resulting the replacement of the coordinating ions. Hence, the white-to-blue shift can be attributed to the dissociation of the lanthanide ions, quenching the red and green emission. Interestingly, the blue emission from the CDs was quenched by both Cu2+ and Fe3+. However, the quenching mechanism of the ions is different. The addition of Cu2+ causes the formation of UV-absorbing copper(II) amine complex on the unreacted amine groups on the CDs' surface, as well as the amine groups in the PEI [15]. This creates a double-shield on the CDs and quenches out most of the blue emission (a small peak is detected on PL, Fig. 7). On the other hand, the complete quenching of the blue emission by Fe3+ is achieved by the fast electron transfer between the CDs and Fe3+. Fe3+ can be absorbed to the surface of CDs and coordinate with phenolic hydroxyl groups on it. This coordination interaction allows nonradiative electron-transfer, from the excited state in the CDs to the unfilled d-orbital of Fe3+ [16,31]. However, this quenching is not observed on the LPPH hydrogel immersed in Fe2+ solution. The coordination to the phenolic hydroxyl groups are unique to Fe3+ and not observed in Fe2+ [31]. Hence, there is no electron-transfer, and no quenching occurs.

In conclusion, we had successfully fabricated a tough LPPH hydrogel using PPH network incorporated with CDs, Eu3+ and Tb3+ that is highly responsiveness towards both pH and metal ions. The colorimetric response for pH is not obvious visually but can be easily tracked by using the PL's intensities. The fabricated LPPH hydrogel displayed distinct differences when sensing between alkaline (remains white) and transition (turns blue) metal ions. Adding on, it can also response differently, no longer luminescent, to metal ions like Cu2+ and Fe3+ as they are also able to quench the blue emission from CDs, which most metal ions are unable to. Intriguingly, the quenching mechanism enables the differentiation of Fe2+ and Fe3+. The fabricated tough LPPH hydrogel did not adhered to swelling/deformation after the immersions and remains its structural integrity at a temperature below 60 °C. These properties make it a good candidate for pH and multiple chemicals sensing applications.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors confirm that there are no known conflicts of interest associated with this publication and there has been no significant financial support for this work that could have influenced its outcome.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Praveen, V. K.; Ranjith, C.; Armaroli, N. White-Light-Emitting Supramolecular Gels. Angew. Chemie - Int. Ed. 2014, 53 (2), 365–368. doi:10.1002/anie.201306787.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Enhanced resonance energy transfer and white-light emission from organic fluorophores and lanthanides in dendron-based hybrid hydrogel

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kumar, P.; Soumya, S.; Prasad, E. Enhanced Resonance Energy Transfer and White-Light Emission from Organic Fluorophores and Lanthanides in Dendron-Based Hybrid Hydrogel. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2016, 8 (12), 8068–8075. doi:10.1021/acsami.6b00018.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single chromophore-based white-light-emitting hydrogel with tunable fluorescence and patternability

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhu, C. N.; Bai, T.; Wang, H.; Bai, W.; Ling, J.; Sun, J. Z.; Huang, F.; Wu, Z. L.; Zheng, Q. Single Chromophore-Based White-Light-Emitting Hydrogel with Tunable Fluorescence and Patternability. ACS Appl. Mater. Interfaces 2018, 10 (45), 39343–39352. doi:10.1021/acsami.8b12619.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: White-light-emitting lanthanide metallogels with tunable luminescence and reversible stimuli-responsive properties

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Chen, P.; Li, Q.; Grindy, S.; Holten-Andersen, N. White-Light-Emitting Lanthanide Metallogels with Tunable Luminescence and Reversible Stimuli-Responsive Properties. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2015, 137 (36), 11590–11593. doi:10.1021/jacs.5b07394.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide based white-light-emitting hydrogel mediated by fluorescein and carbon dots with high quantum yield and multi-stimuli responsiveness

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Xue, J.; Xu, X.; Zhu, Y.; Yang, D. Lanthanide Based White-Light-Emitting Hydrogel Mediated by Fluorescein and Carbon Dots with High Quantum Yield and Multi-Stimuli Responsiveness. J. Mater. Chem. C 2020, 8 (10), 3380–3385. doi:10.1039/c9tc06590c.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide-containing light-emitting organic-inorganic hybrids: a bet on the future

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Carlos, L. D.; Ferreira, R. A. S.; De Zea Bermudez, V.; Ribeiro, S. J. L. Lanthanide-Containing Light-Emitting Organic-Inorganic Hybrids: A Bet on the Future. Adv. Mater. 2009, 21 (5), 509–534. doi:10.1002/adma.200801635.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electroluminescent materials for white organic light emitting diodes

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Farinola, G. M.; Ragni, R. Electroluminescent Materials for White Organic Light Emitting Diodes. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2011, 40 (7), 3467–3482. doi:10.1039/c0cs00204f.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Recent advances in white organic light-emitting materials and devices (WOLEDS)

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Kamtekar, K. T.; Monkman, A. P.; Bryce, M. R. Recent Advances in White Organic Light-Emitting Materials and Devices (WOLEDS). Adv. Mater. 2010, 22 (5), 572–582. doi:10.1002/adma.200902148.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Progress and perspective of polymer white light-emitting devices and materials

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Wu, H.; Ying, L.; Yang, W.; Cao, Y. Progress and Perspective of Polymer White Light-Emitting Devices and Materials. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2009, 38 (12), 3391–3400. doi:10.1039/b816352a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Tunable white-light emission by supramolecular self-sorting in highly swollen hydrogels

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Zhao, Q.; Chen, Y.; Li, S. H.; Liu, Y. Tunable White-Light Emission by Supramolecular Self-Sorting in Highly Swollen Hydrogels. Chem. Commun. 2017, 54 (2), 200–203. doi:10.1039/c7cc08822a.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Protein biophosphors: biodegradable, multifunctional, protein-based hydrogel for white emission, sensing, and PH detection

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Benson, K.; Ghimire, A.; Pattammattel, A.; Kumar, C. V. Protein Biophosphors: Biodegradable, Multifunctional, Protein-Based Hydrogel for White Emission, Sensing, and PH Detection. Adv. Funct. Mater. 2017, 27 (43), 1–11. doi:10.1002/adfm.201702955.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Lanthanide luminescence for biomedical analyses and imaging

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Bünzli, J. C. G. Lanthanide Luminescence for Biomedical Analyses and Imaging. Chem. Rev. 2010, 110 (5), 2729–2755. doi:10.1021/cr900362e.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Bottom-up fabrication of photoluminescent graphene quantum dots with uniform morphology

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Liu, R.; Wu, D.; Feng, X.; Müllen, K. Bottom-up Fabrication of Photoluminescent Graphene Quantum Dots with Uniform Morphology. J. Am. Chem. Soc. 2011, 133 (39), 15221–15223. doi:10.1021/ja204953k.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Carbon quantum dots and their applications

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Lim, S. Y.; Shen, W.; Gao, Z. Carbon Quantum Dots and Their Applications. Chem. Soc. Rev. 2015, 44 (1), 362–381. doi:10.1039/c4cs00269e.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Polyamine-functionalized carbon quantum dots as fluorescent probes for selective and sensitive detection of copper ions

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Dong, Y.; Wang, R.; Li, G.; Chen, C.; Chi, Y.; Chen, G. Polyamine-Functionalized Carbon Quantum Dots as Fluorescent Probes for Selective and Sensitive Detection of Copper Ions. Anal. Chem. 2012, 84 (14), 6220–6224. doi:10.1021/ac3012126.
